item.content:encoded: Bounced here after reading Slimelord. You're really good at writing alien dialogue, please do more!
Got to +100! Thank you all for reading :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I'm glad you enjoyed it!

I was waffling around the Object Class, but ultimately decided that because it can effectively use some sort of unblockable weapon against the earth, though a very specialized one that probably can only damage the right kind of scientific equipment, it should be classified as Keter. Even though its intentions aren't hostile, it still does have that capability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all I love the article, and want more! It got awws and giggles. +1

Second
I'm not an expert or anything but I think there is there's plenty of space SCPs that while the foundation can't put in a box, they consider it contained if nobody else knows about it and it isn't changing that. This guy seems like he might be Euclid, possessing the ability to break containment, but generally not actively doing so like Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that I think about it, the Foundation doesn't know what this skip is capable of and the neutrino beam is something it did casually. Yeah, it should stay Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, Keter doesn’t mean “dangerous.” It’s keter because it’s uncontained, and uncontainable with the technology available to the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So what makes this Keter? The concentrated neutrino beams it made that could arouse suspicion amongst the scientific community? It's only done it once and the red dot it perceived is mostly likely the CHANT device which isn't working anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
is this reverse 17776

+1 for making me feel emotions

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, Doctor Bright! I'm honored :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have tears in my eyes, this was an amazing article and I really want a sequel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback! Part of the in-universe translation process for their language involves putting their abstract concepts into human naming conventions. While dark matter creatures undoubtedly have their own terminology for the way they move across the Higgs field, it seemed logical that the Foundation would put it into terms familiar to their researchers and translate their naming conventions into ours, when there is an unambiguous one-to-one relation between the two.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love how nonplussed the Foundation is regarding sapiemt darkmatter, so much so that it's not even anomalous by their standards.

I wish that this stayed focused on interactions between DMIs, primarily due to how the speech was handled: it's not perfect, but it sufficiently captures the portrayal of an alien intelligence much better than is typically seen, though a large part of this is lost in the assertations, where it veers away from approximated translations as can be understood by humans and into direct dialogue - a weakness I'm willing to overlook only because the technical writing was otherwise sound.

To nitpick, it seems odd that it would use the term 'Higgs field' I would think that it has its own frame of reference for that — not something tied to our naming conventions.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Thank you! That tale is definitely in my notes file, and I am hoping to sit down and write it in a couple of months. I'm toying with the idea of writing it mostly from the Translator's point of view (the Foundation researcher who annotates the communications logs in this article) interspersed with vignettes from the child's POV, with the plot of the story being how the child's life progresses over the course of several decades, as seen from the outside.

If you told me someone wrote a skip about sapient dark matter intelligence in our solar system, I would assume that the tone would be dark.

After reading, I'm genuinely tickled that they seem to be not only benevolent, but utterly innocent and childlike. This skip is damn refreshing. Personally, I'd love a tale focused on the person (child?) that 4170 is speaking with.

I was untruthful when we first met. I told you I was not lonely, and that was a lie]. [Assertion: I had never had a friend until I met you]. [Assertion: Even though your parents are unkind, they somehow managed to create the kindest person in the Solar System].

^That right there. That shit made me tear up. Bravo and +1.

Edit: I'm genuinely curious about the only -1 rating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, I really liked this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Starman, waiting in the sky. He’d like to come to meet us, but he’s scared he’ll blow our minds.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just read SCP-3201, and the first word that came to mind was "glorious." I'm flattered by the comparison, but I don't think my writing is quite that good!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! This piece was a pleasure to write, and I'm glad it's just as much of a pleasure to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is remarkably well-written.

The technical aspects give us science nerds a lot to look into without being overwhelmed. The sci-fi spices are effectively-placed, and are sophisticated in their restraint: the last sentence of the first paragraph, that the Foundation doesn't regard the mere existence of DBIs themselves as anomalous, the concept of a scientifically-plausible evolution of sapient dark-matter beings, that we are observing "mundane" acts of extraterrestrial intelligence without their knowing, etc. The prose is so polished that it can put the line telling us why the Foundation actually considers this anomalous deep into the description, and not only get away with it, but be a pleasure to get to.

The particular human contact gives this an emotional sweetness which, thanks to the contrast of the technical detail and the originality in the remainder of the article, doesn't feel like it's all the article relies on.

It reminds me of xplkqlkcassia1 in the best of ways, and may even be better.

Footnotes
1. Example work: SCP-3201

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saw this in the ideas and brainstorming iirc. Really cool how it turned out! Something about personified dark matter is just a massive win for me. +1

item.title: Re: Image Credit
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I figured that because there is very little to do out in space beyond chat with each other, I would make some fun social dynamics the focus of this article. "Passive aggressive invisible aliens" seemed like a good choice, so I ran with it!

item.title: Re: Image Credit
item.content:encoded: Enjoyed it as a draft, enjoying it now. I didn't say it before, but I love the dark matter entities being passive aggressive and weirdly emotionally manipulative with each other.

item.title: Image Credit
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4170 image is adapted from a picture in the NASA-JPL Photo Journal, released without a license and free to adapt for non-commercial purposes.

I couldn't figure out a good way to work it into the article, but "DBI" is pronounced "Debby" in casual conversation.
